questions.question: Explain the sequence of events during a memory READ operation.
keyPoints: CPU places the memory address on the address bus
CPU asserts the READ control signal
Memory fetches data from the specified address
Memory places data on the data bus
CPU reads data from data bus into internal register

questions.question: Explain the sequence of events during a memory WRITE operation.
keyPoints: CPU places the memory address on the address bus
CPU places the data to be written on the data bus
CPU asserts the WRITE control signal
Memory receives and stores the data at the specified address
Data is written into the memory location

questions.question: Define and differentiate between big-endian and little-endian byte ordering.
keyPoints: Big-endian: most significant byte stored at lowest address
Little-endian: least significant byte stored at lowest address
Big-endian is more natural for human reading
Little-endian simplifies arithmetic operations
Important for data exchange between different systems

questions.question: Explain the concepts of temporal locality and spatial locality with examples.
keyPoints: Temporal locality: recently accessed items accessed again (loop variables)
Spatial locality: nearby locations accessed (array traversal)
Forms basis for cache memory design
Programs exhibit both types of locality
Examples: looping through array, function recursion

questions.question: Describe the operations of PUSH and POP in a stack memory.
keyPoints: PUSH: decrement stack pointer, store data at pointed location
POP: retrieve data from pointed location, increment stack pointer
Stack typically grows downward in memory
Used for function call management and temporary storage
Stack pointer always points to top of stack

questions.question: Explain the memory hierarchy and its importance in computer system design.
keyPoints: Hierarchy: Registers, Cache, Main Memory, Secondary Storage
Each level differs in speed, size, and cost per bit
Faster memory is more expensive, so smaller
Goal: provide illusion of fast, large memory
Modern systems use multiple cache levels (L1, L2, L3)

questions.question: Explain the difference between cache memory and virtual memory.
keyPoints: Cache: hardware solution between CPU and RAM
Virtual memory: software/hardware solution using disk
Cache reduces average access time
Virtual memory extends available address space
Both use principle of locality

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the data flow between CPU, memory, and I/O devices during read and write operations.
keyPoints: Address bus carries memory addresses from CPU to memory
Data bus carries actual data between CPU and memory
Control bus carries read/write signals
CPU initiates operation by placing address and control signals
Memory responds with data (read) or acknowledges write

questions.question: Explain the concept of word alignment. Why is aligned memory access faster than unaligned access?
keyPoints: Word alignment: words stored at addresses multiple of word size
Unaligned access requires multiple memory operations
Aligned access: single memory operation fetches entire word
Processors are optimized for aligned access
Misaligned access may cause performance penalty or hardware exceptions

questions.question: Explain how memory addressing modes work, specifically focusing on memory-direct and register-indirect addressing.
keyPoints: Direct addressing: address is explicitly given in instruction
Register indirect: address is stored in a register
Memory direct: instruction contains memory address directly
Register indirect: register contains memory address to access
Effective address calculation differs for each mode
